title: Audit Storage Capacity and Index Growth Guardrails

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 AU-4 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.3: Audit Storage Capacity and Index Growth Guardrails.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.14.3' running on _internal (metrics.log license), /services/data/indexes (index size, frozenTimePeriodInSecs), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform (REST, DMC)
spl:
| rest /services/data/indexes splunk_server=local count=0
| search disabled=0 NOT title IN ("_*")
| eval size_gb=currentDBSizeMB/1024
| eval retention_days=round(frozenTimePeriodInSecs/86400,1)
| table title, size_gb, maxTotalDataSizeMB, retention_days, frozenTimePeriodInSecs
| sort - size_gb
description: Detects indexes approaching disk or license limits that could cause loss of audit evidence, supporting AU-4 audit storage capacity management.
value: Detects indexes approaching disk or license limits that could cause loss of audit evidence, supporting AU-4 audit storage capacity management.

grandmaExplanation: We catch data approaching disk or license limits that could cause loss of audit evidence, supporting AU-4 audit storage capacity management — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform (REST, DMC).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `_internal` (metrics.log license), `/services/data/indexes` (index size, frozenTimePeriodInSecs).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Run from a scheduled search with `rest` rights; (2) compare `size_gb` to business thresholds by index class; (3) integrate license usage from `_internal` for combined risk scoring; (4) open change requests before forced rolling.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| rest /services/data/indexes splunk_server=local count=0
| search disabled=0 NOT title IN ("_*")
| eval size_gb=currentDBSizeMB/1024
| eval retention_days=round(frozenTimePeriodInSecs/86400,1)
| table title, size_gb, maxTotalDataSizeMB, retention_days, frozenTimePeriodInSecs
| sort - size_gb
```

Understanding this SPL

**Audit Storage Capacity and Index Growth Guardrails (AU-4)** — Detects indexes approaching disk or license limits that could cause loss of audit evidence, supporting AU-4 audit storage capacity management.

Documented **Data sources**: `_internal` (metrics.log license), `/services/data/indexes` (index size, frozenTimePeriodInSecs). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform (REST, DMC). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Calls Splunk `rest` to read configuration or REST-exposed entities.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **size_gb** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **retention_days** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Pipeline stage (see **Audit Storage Capacity and Index Growth Guardrails (AU-4)**): table title, size_gb, maxTotalDataSizeMB, retention_days, frozenTimePeriodInSecs
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Single value (indexes over threshold), Table (top consumers), Column chart (size by index)
